skill.name: building-agents
skill.description: Create, update, validate, and standardize Claude Code subagents with templates, checklists, migration guidance, and a validation script.
skill.summary: Build and maintain Claude Code subagents with structured guidance and validation.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: The static scanner reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are markdown examples, checklists, color swatch URLs, or relative documentation links. No prompt-injection attempt, malware intent, credential exfiltration, or live dynamic code execution was found. Publication is reasonable with a warning because this community skill can write and edit agent files and has Bash permission.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions for Agent Authoring
medium_findings.description: The skill frontmatter grants Write, Edit, and Bash, and the guidance covers project, user, and plugin agent file locations. This is legitimate for an agent-building skill, but it can modify agent behavior and should be used with user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool permissions and agent file paths are explicit in the skill. The risk is bounded by the stated agent-authoring purpose, so this is elevated but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Command Execution Example May Be Copied Unsafely
medium_findings.description: A reference section demonstrates an unsafe os.system call using user input before showing a mitigated version with shlex.quote. Because this appears in documentation rather than live code, the risk is educational-copy risk rather than direct execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe command execution example is directly present and clearly marked as bad. It is not executed by the skill, which lowers confidence that it creates a direct vulnerability.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Execution and Weak Crypto Hits Are Checklist Text
low_findings.description: Static hits for eval(), exec(), and weak cryptography occur in a security-auditor checklist that tells users what to flag. I did not find evidence that this skill executes eval() or exec().
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms are inside a markdown checklist, not active code. The surrounding context is defensive security review guidance.

low_findings.title: External URLs Are Color Swatch Images
low_findings.description: Static network findings point to placehold.co image URLs used in a color palette reference. These are hardcoded external resources, but they do not send secrets or run code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible markdown image links for color samples. No credential, environment, or user data transmission was found.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Hits Are Relative Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Static path traversal findings mostly come from markdown links to neighboring reference files and a warning that users should validate paths. I did not find evidence of traversal used for file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ../ sequences are documentation links or examples of what to prevent. The validator script reads a user-supplied path but does not use traversal strings from these markdown references.

low_findings.title: Validator Reads User-Supplied Files Without Execution
low_findings.description: The validation script reads the file path supplied on the command line and parses YAML with yaml.safe_load. This can inspect arbitrary local files chosen by the user, but no execution or exfiltration behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-read behavior is explicit and expected for validation. The use of yaml.safe_load and absence of network calls or subprocess execution make direct abuse less likely.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented os.system Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference material includes an os.system example to teach command injection prevention. It is not active skill code, but users should avoid copying shell-based patterns into generated agents without stronger subprocess safeguards.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact dangerous pattern is present in a markdown example. The file context labels it as bad and provides mitigation, so the direct execution risk is low.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent Filesystem Modification Capability
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is designed to create or update agent markdown files, including user-level Claude agent paths. This is expected functionality, but generated agent changes should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file locations are directly documented. The purpose is legitimate, but modifications under agent directories can affect future AI behavior.

content.value_statement: Creating consistent Claude Code agents is hard when schemas, tools, and quality checks vary. This skill provides templates, update guidance, validation, and review checklists for reliable agent development.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use an agent, skill, or command for Claude Code workflows.
Provides standard YAML frontmatter and markdown structure for subagent files.
Guides updates to agent names, descriptions, tools, models, colors, and capabilities.
Includes a Python validator for agent frontmatter and common schema issues.
Provides templates, examples, migration guidance, and quality review checklists.
Documents security considerations for tool permissions, Bash use, and secrets.
limitations: It focuses on Claude Code subagent files, not general autonomous agent frameworks.
It can suggest Bash-enabled agents, so generated permissions need human review.
The validator checks structure and conventions, not runtime behavior or model quality.
Some examples are documentation patterns and may need adaptation before production use.

use_cases.title: Create a new project subagent
use_cases.description: Generate a focused agent file with valid frontmatter, clear workflow steps, and scoped tool permissions.

use_cases.title: Standardize an agent library
use_cases.description: Review existing agents for naming, descriptions, capabilities, model choices, and consistent color usage.

use_cases.title: Audit agent quality before release
use_cases.description: Use the checklist and validator to find schema, permission, documentation, and maintainability gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new subagent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code agent for reviewing API documentation. Use minimal tools and explain when it should be invoked.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an existing agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate update to a current agent file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent file for schema quality, description clarity, tool permissions, model choice, and missing capabilities.

prompt_templates.title: Design an agent set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for multiple related agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a small set of Claude Code agents for a testing workflow. Define responsibilities, boundaries, tools, colors, and invocation descriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate and validate agents
prompt_templates.prompt: Update these agents to the current recommended schema, add capabilities, reduce excessive tools, and run validation checks where appropriate.

output_examples.input: Create an agent for pull request review.
output_examples.output: A complete agent plan with a lowercase name, scoped description, capabilities, minimal tools, model recommendation, and review workflow.

output_examples.input: Check whether this existing agent is ready to use.
Build a coordinated agent architecture for release work.
output_examples.output: A division of responsibilities across focused agents, with guidance to use a skill for orchestration rather than nested subagents.

best_practices: Keep each agent focused on one domain and give it only the tools it needs.
Write invocation descriptions that clearly state when Claude should use the agent.
Validate generated agent files and review Bash or write permissions before deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not give every agent Bash, Write, or broad tool access by default.
Do not use subagents as orchestrators that need to spawn other subagents.
Do not publish generated agents without checking names, descriptions, tools, and secrets.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create and update Claude Code subagent markdown files with YAML frontmatter and structured instructions.

faq.question: Does it run agents for me?
faq.answer: No. It helps design, edit, and validate agent files. Runtime behavior depends on Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it modify user-level agent files?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation covers user-level paths, so users should approve changes before applying them.

faq.question: Does the validator execute agent content?
faq.answer: No. The validator reads a file, parses YAML with safe loading, and checks conventions.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it has Bash and file editing capability and can change future agent behavior.

faq.question: Is it useful outside Claude Code?
faq.answer: It is mainly for Claude Code. Codex users can still use the templates and review guidance for similar workflows.
